utterances: So what questions does this story raise in your mind, given that the long path of segregation in baseball - and sport in general - continued so long after this?
One question it raises is - in my mind - is why this team was not a little better known than it was.
I mean, they accomplished something quite remarkable.
The question you can always ask is why didn't it happen earlier?
But there were not enough Neil Churchills pushing that.
It was a slow grind.
And this was an important step in that process of grinding forward.
And I understand there's a single photograph of this integrated team.
It's quite interesting.
There's one photograph that was taken by a newspaper photographer before the team left for this big Wichita tournament in August of 1935.
And you can see he arranged the players.
Infielders are in front with Churchill, which is mostly white players.
The outfielder and the pitchers are in the back.
And there's one white person in that back row, who's the left fielder, Moose Johnson, the power-hitting left fielder.
He is standing next to Satchel Paige - Satchel is on his right - and Moose has his big paw of a right hand on Satchel's shoulder.
That hand on the shoulder is a precursor of an iconic baseball moment which occurred early in the spring of 1947.
The Dodgers were playing in Cincinnati.
Pee Wee Reese is standing by second base with Jackie Robinson, and he puts his arm around Robinson's shoulder.
That was Pee Wee Reese signaling to his Dodger teammates and to the rest of the country that Jackie Robinson is my teammate.
I accept him.
This is all going to be OK.
And back in 1935 in Bismarck, for a white player to just casually have his hand on Satchel Paige's shoulder says something.
Try to find another picture like that.
That's writer Tom Dunkel.
His new book is called ""Color Blind: The Forgotten Team that Broke Baseball's Color Line."" Tom, thanks for being here.
Thank you for having me.
This is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Melissa Block.
And I'm Audie Cornish.
7.7 percent is the latest unemployment rate.
That's the number for February, according to the Labor Department's report out this morning.
Economists were expecting a ho-hum job survey.
Instead, they got a pleasant surprise, as NPR's Dan Bobkoff reports.
There's a lot to like in these jobs numbers.
The U.S.
gained 236,000 jobs in February, far more than anticipated.
Many thought the unemployment rate would hold steady at 7.9 percent.
Instead, it dropped two-tenths of a percent, to levels not seen since late 2008.
I thought it was an excellent report.
Brian Jones is a senior economist at Societe Generale in New York.
I think it's a sign of not only an improving economy, but a labor market that's starting to gather some steam.
The job increases were across the board.
Health care has long been a bright spot and it gained jobs, but so did hard hit sectors like retail and construction.
In Lincoln, Nebraska, Mike Rezac builds custom homes as president of Rezac Construction.
He's seen housing's rebound firsthand.
We did more work last year than we've done in 35 years of business.
Construction was one of the worst hit sectors during the economic crisis, but it gained 151,000 jobs since September, about a third of those just last month.
For all workers, the average work week rose slightly, as did average hourly earnings.
Alan Krueger, the top White House economist, says President Obama liked what he saw.
As a general matter, I don't discuss my meetings with the president, but I did for the first time get a fist bump.
Krueger says the report is a good sign for the recovery.
We're not all the way back, but we have done a lot of healing.
Today's numbers don't take into account the across-the-board budget cuts known as the sequester.
Those took effect March 1st and are expected to cut economic growth.
Cary Leahey, of Decision Economics, agrees today's report is positive.
But he warned that the sequester, combined with higher payroll taxes, could change things.
The worry, of course, is that maybe things will unravel a bit as we deal with the restraint from the government sector, but it's certainly not a blip.
Holmes says the stakes are high when it comes to flood risk.
In many parts of the country, flooding is getting more frequent and severe.
Climate change is part of the problem.
Warmer air can hold more moisture, which falls as more extreme rain.
And in many places, development is also creating more runoff, all of which puts more people in harm's way, many of whom don't know they're at risk.
You know, if you build in the wrong spot or you buy a house that, you know, you were unaware that you had a risk, you know, you could lose your life savings.
Or worst case, you lose a member of your family or your own life.
Rebecca Hersher, NPR News.
(SOUNDBITE OF FAYE WEBSTER SONG ""SHE WON'T GO AWAY"")
From the studios of NPR West, this is Day to Day.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
And I'm Madeleine Brand.
Coming up, a mortgage crisis rekindles true love.
First, inflation is up, business is sluggish, the markets are shaky.
However you look at it, the economy is having troubles.
So what's next?
How about credit cards?
People do tend to carry some pretty big debt on their plastic.
Producer Ki-Min Sung talked with some shoppers in Santa Monica.
I started with about 15,000, and right now struggling with about 3,000 dollars.
What was the largest credit card purchase?
Car.
We bought a Nissan.
That saved - about five, six thousand dollars gone there.
My credit card debt is, like, 11,000 dollars.
And what's the most expensive thing you've ever charged?
Recently it was a plasma TV, 42 inch.
And how much does that cost?
Twelve hundred, maybe?
I have about 600 dollars.
I only use it for gas, and the way gas is, you know, driving all around.
(Soundbite of laughter)
So I just use it for gas.
Honestly.
Those are voices of some credit card holders here in southern California.
That was Adam Moss, and Kilda Larkin (ph), and Artie Agerwald (ph).
So, is a credit card crisis next?
Ron Ianieri is the chief market strategist for an investor education company.
It's called Options University.
What's going on is, is as inflation increases, more and more of us are spending more and more dollars on necessities.
So what's happening is, is our disposable income is getting cut down, and our necessity income is increasing.
As our disposable income gets knocked down, we're going to look to replace that.
And normally, what happens is, is people turn to credit cards to replace that disposable income.
So is the danger that Americans will just stop paying their credit card bills and default?
That is exactly what the danger is, and that's exactly what we're seeing right now.
We're seeing an increase in defaults on credit cards, but we're also seeing the highest level we've ever seen in terms of late payments.
Most amount of people, biggest amount of dollars in terms of late payments.
And that has been increasing for the last several months.
So, if you default on your mortgage, you lose your house.
What happens if you default on your credit card?
Well, if you default on your credit card, eventually they're going to ask for the entire balance to be paid immediately.
That's what winds up happening.
From there, you become a credit risk.
So whether you - you know, if you don't pay off that balance, the entire balance, immediately, it starts to show on your credit report.
In the same way, we're seeing a lot of banks and their credit ratings decrease, which makes it harder for them to borrow money.
The same thing happens to individual people.
As your credit risk increases, the likelihood and the chances of you being able to borrow money become much more unlikely.
And in a situation and a time where we already have a problem with getting credit, it's going to make it real tough for a lot of people to get money.
So, Ron, broaden this out if you would, and talk about how this would affect the larger economy, banks in particular.
We've seen a number of them go under because of the housing crisis.
Could the same thing happen because of a credit card crisis?
Well, I don't think necessarily that they hold as much credit card debt as they would hold mortgage debt.

However, in a situation where they're already in trouble from mortgage debt, adding a credit card debt problem with these banks, a failure to collect there, could be the straw that breaks the camel's back.
So it's more of a thing where I don't think that the actual credit card crisis itself is that destructive, but when you combine it with the mortgage crisis and the overall credit crisis, yes.
This could be the final blow to a lot of different banks.
